source_file: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

heuristics.rule: Secure the offer letter and confirm start date before resigning
heuristics.condition: When changing jobs during OPT and trying to avoid unemployment day accumulation
heuristics.action: Get a signed offer letter with a confirmed start date first, then submit resignation, then notify DSO the same day
heuristics.because: The poster's second job change succeeded using this sequence; the first failed because they resigned first and accumulated 95 days of unemployment, exceeding the 90-day cumulative limit

heuristics.rule: Notify DSO within 10 days of starting a new job to update SEVIS
heuristics.condition: When any job change occurs during OPT (standard or STEM)
heuristics.action: Contact DSO on or before day 10 after the employment change to ensure SEVIS record is updated
heuristics.because: Federal regulation requires SEVIS records reflect current employer; failure creates compliance risk. Multiple comments and the original poster confirm this as a hard requirement

heuristics.rule: For STEM OPT job changes, ensure E-Verify registration and I-983 are completed before your first day
heuristics.condition: When switching employers on STEM OPT extension
heuristics.action: Confirm new employer is E-Verify registered and submit I-983 Training Plan prior to start date, not after
heuristics.because: The original poster explicitly flags this as a common error; STEM OPT authorization depends on employer compliance being in place before work begins

heuristics.rule: Always consult your DSO before changing jobs on OPT
heuristics.condition: When considering any job change during OPT period
heuristics.action: Contact DSO proactively before making any employment decisions
heuristics.because: DSO intervention helped the original poster recover from a compliance error; multiple comments reinforce that assumptions about what's allowed are dangerous

decision_factors.why_it_matters: OPT has a 90-day cumulative unemployment cap; exceeding it jeopardizes OPT status and can trigger RFE or denial during H-1B adjudication
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Track total days unemployed since OPT start date, including all gaps across all job changes, not just the most recent one

decision_factors.factor: New employer's E-Verify status (STEM OPT only)
decision_factors.why_it_matters: STEM OPT authorization requires the employer to be enrolled in E-Verify; working for a non-enrolled employer invalidates STEM OPT
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask the employer directly and verify through the E-Verify employer search tool before accepting the offer

decision_factors.factor: Professional field alignment between new job and degree
decision_factors.why_it_matters: OPT requires employment to be directly related to the field of study; a mismatch can invalidate OPT authorization
decision_factors.how_to_assess: DSO must assess and update SEVIS with the new employer; consult DSO if the field alignment is ambiguous

decision_factors.factor: Buffer days between resignation and new start date
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The gap between leaving one job and starting the next counts toward the 90-day cumulative unemployment limit
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate the difference between last day at old employer and first day at new employer; minimize this window

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Resigning before securing a new offer to focus on job searching
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Every day without employment counts toward the 90-day cumulative OPT unemployment cap, which is tracked across the entire OPT period — not just the current gap
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: OPT holders who assume the 90-day limit resets with each job or applies only to a single continuous gap

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Doing freelance or contract work between jobs to fill income gaps
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: It is unclear whether freelance/self-employment counts as 'employed' for OPT unemployment calculation purposes; this ambiguity can create compliance risk if USCIS or DSO does not recognize it
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: OPT holders doing independent consulting or gig work while job searching, who assume it protects them from the unemployment clock

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Exceeding the 90-day unemployment limit without knowing it
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The violation can surface during H-1B RFE adjudication, long after OPT has ended, creating retroactive immigration consequences
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone who had a prolonged job search gap on OPT and later applies for H-1B status

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Submitting I-983 after starting work at a new STEM OPT employer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Work performed before I-983 is submitted and E-Verify enrollment is confirmed may be unauthorized, creating a status violation even if the employer is eventually compliant
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: STEM OPT holders whose new employers are cooperative but unaware of the pre-start-date requirement

failure_patterns.pattern: Resigning first, then job searching — accumulating unemployment days over the 90-day limit
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat OPT unemployment limits like domestic employment norms, where taking time off between jobs is routine and consequence-free
failure_patterns.consequence: Original poster accumulated 95 days of unemployment, exceeding the limit; required DSO intervention; caused significant anxiety and immigration risk
warning_signs: Planning to 'take a break' before starting the job search
Not tracking cumulative unemployment days across all previous gaps in the OPT period

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming the 90-day unemployment limit applies per gap, not cumulatively
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The word 'limit' implies a single-instance rule; the cumulative nature is not intuitive and is not prominently communicated by most schools
failure_patterns.consequence: Students unknowingly exceed the total allowance by treating each gap as starting fresh; this was confirmed by a highly upvoted comment (134 likes) describing an H-1B RFE triggered by a 4-month gap
warning_signs: Saying 'I've only been unemployed for X days this time' without accounting for prior gaps
Not asking DSO for a running total of unemployment days

failure_patterns.pattern: Delaying SEVIS update after starting a new job during OPT
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on onboarding at the new job and deprioritize administrative tasks; the 10-day window is shorter than most bureaucratic timelines feel
failure_patterns.consequence: SEVIS record remains inaccurate, creating a compliance gap that can affect H-1B petitions or future visa applications
warning_signs: Starting a new job without immediately contacting DSO
Assuming the new employer handles the SEVIS update

failure_patterns.pattern: Starting STEM OPT work before I-983 and E-Verify are complete
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: New employers often do not know the pre-start requirement; students assume documentation can be completed during onboarding like I-9 paperwork
failure_patterns.consequence: Work performed before documentation is in place may be considered unauthorized employment under STEM OPT rules
warning_signs: Employer says 'we'll sort the paperwork out once you start'
I-983 not signed before the first day of work

diagnostic_questions.question: How many total unemployment days have you accumulated since your OPT start date, across all job gaps combined?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is at risk of exceeding the 90-day cumulative cap, and how much buffer they actually have
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If close to 90 days, ask: do you have a confirmed offer with a start date already secured?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If far from 90 days, standard job-change process applies; focus on DSO notification timing

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your new employer enrolled in E-Verify?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether STEM OPT employment is valid at all; a non-enrolled employer disqualifies STEM OPT authorization
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Has the I-983 Training Plan been signed and submitted before your start date?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Do not begin work; require E-Verify enrollment before your start date or reconsider the offer

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you already resigned, or do you still have your current job?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether unemployment days are already accumulating; determines urgency of finding next role
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Calculate days elapsed since resignation; if approaching 90 cumulative, contact DSO immediately
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Good position — secure offer first before resigning to minimize gap

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your new role clearly align with your degree field of study?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether DSO can approve the SEVIS update; a misaligned role may not qualify for OPT employment
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Proceed with standard DSO notification process
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Consult DSO before accepting the offer; do not assume the role qualifies

industry_realities.common_belief: The 90-day unemployment limit on OPT resets or applies separately to each job gap
industry_realities.actual_reality: The 90-day limit is cumulative across the entire OPT period; all unemployment gaps since OPT start date are added together
industry_realities.implication: Students must track total unemployment days from OPT start, not just the current gap; even someone who never had a long single gap can exceed the limit through multiple short ones

industry_realities.common_belief: Changing jobs on OPT requires re-applying for a new EAD or restarting the OPT process
industry_realities.actual_reality: The OPT EAD is not employer-specific; it does not need to be reissued when changing jobs. Only SEVIS needs to be updated by the DSO
industry_realities.implication: Students should not delay job changes out of fear of losing their EAD; the administrative burden is DSO notification, not USCIS re-filing

industry_realities.common_belief: OPT unemployment violations only matter during the OPT period itself
industry_realities.actual_reality: OPT compliance history can be scrutinized during H-1B adjudication; a 4-month gap on OPT triggered an H-1B RFE for at least one commenter (134 likes)
industry_realities.implication: OPT violations have long-tail immigration consequences; students should treat OPT compliance as part of their overall immigration record, not a temporary status with no downstream effects

industry_realities.common_belief: For STEM OPT, I-983 can be submitted after starting work as long as the employer is E-Verify enrolled
industry_realities.actual_reality: I-983 and E-Verify enrollment must be in place before the first day of work, not during onboarding
industry_realities.implication: STEM OPT holders must proactively push employers to complete documentation before start date; beginning work early creates an unauthorized employment risk

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Tracks days in the current gap only; assumes each job change resets the counter; does not ask DSO for cumulative total
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Tracks cumulative unemployment days from OPT start date; factors in all prior gaps; builds in buffer when planning next job change
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A student who has had two short gaps earlier may unknowingly be near the limit; only cumulative tracking reveals actual risk

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Resigns first to 'focus on the search' or to avoid awkward overlap with current employer
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Secures signed offer with confirmed start date before submitting resignation; minimizes the gap window to a deliberate, counted buffer
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Every day between jobs counts toward the cap; informed sequencing can be the difference between compliance and violation

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes documentation (I-983, E-Verify) can be handled during the first weeks of work, like standard HR onboarding
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Confirms E-Verify enrollment and submits I-983 before the first day; treats this as a pre-condition of the start date, not a post-hire formality
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Working before documentation is complete may constitute unauthorized employment even with a cooperative employer

community_misconceptions.misconception: If you've only been unemployed for less than 90 days in your current gap, you're fine
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The rule is often stated as '90 days maximum unemployment' without the word 'cumulative' being emphasized; each job gap feels like a fresh start
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The 90-day limit is cumulative across the entire OPT period; all gaps since OPT authorization began are counted together
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students who had earlier gaps may unknowingly be at or near the limit; exceeding it can retroactively affect H-1B petitions and future immigration applications

community_misconceptions.misconception: I-983 for STEM OPT can be submitted after you start working, as long as the employer is E-Verify registered
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Employment paperwork (like I-9) is typically completed on or after the first day; students assume immigration documentation follows the same process
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: I-983 and E-Verify enrollment must be completed before the first day of work for STEM OPT to be valid
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Work performed before I-983 submission may be considered unauthorized employment, creating a status violation even if the employer later becomes fully compliant

community_misconceptions.misconception: Freelance or contract work between jobs definitely counts as employment for OPT purposes and stops the unemployment clock
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Students reason that earning income means they are 'employed'; this feels logically consistent with the purpose of OPT
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The question of whether freelance/self-employment qualifies as OPT-authorized employment is ambiguous and contested; it is not a settled safe harbor
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students relying on freelance work to avoid the unemployment clock may be in violation without knowing it; DSO and USCIS may not recognize self-employment as qualifying

real_case_outcomes.situation: OPT holder resigned from first job without a new offer, assumed they had time to search, and ended up with a 95-day unemployment gap
real_case_outcomes.decision: Did not track cumulative days; did not notify DSO proactively; resigned first and searched after
real_case_outcomes.outcome: Exceeded the 90-day cumulative unemployment limit by 5 days; DSO intervened to help remediate but the experience caused significant anxiety and immigration risk
real_case_outcomes.lesson: The 90-day rule is cumulative and unforgiving; even a few extra days over the limit creates a compliance issue. Always secure offer first, resign second, notify DSO immediately

real_case_outcomes.situation: OPT holder had a 4-month gap during their OPT period (assumed it was within limits or not tracked), then later applied for H-1B
real_case_outcomes.decision: Did not treat the OPT gap as a long-term immigration record issue
real_case_outcomes.outcome: Received an H-1B RFE specifically questioning the 4-month unemployment period during OPT
real_case_outcomes.lesson: OPT compliance is part of the permanent immigration record; violations surface during H-1B adjudication even years later. The 90-day limit must be treated as a binding constraint, not a soft guideline

psychological_barriers.trigger: Student asks whether freelance work 'counts' as employment during OPT gap, framing it as a way to avoid the unemployment clock
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Motivated reasoning — seeking confirmation that an already-chosen coping behavior is compliant, rather than genuinely evaluating the risk
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Posts a question hoping for reassurance; may not follow up if the answer is ambiguous or negative; continues freelancing without DSO confirmation
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Redirect: 'The question isn't whether it feels like employment — it's whether your DSO and USCIS will count it. Have you asked your DSO specifically?' Force the student to get an official answer before relying on it

psychological_barriers.trigger: Student is anxious about awkwardness with current employer and wants to resign before having a new offer secured
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Social discomfort avoidance — the discomfort of staying in a job while searching feels more immediate than the abstract risk of exceeding the unemployment cap
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Plans to resign 'to focus on the search' or 'to avoid conflict'; underweights immigration risk relative to interpersonal discomfort
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Make the cost concrete: 'Every day between your last day and your new start date counts toward your 90-day lifetime limit on OPT. If you've had any prior gaps, you may have less buffer than you think. What's your current cumulative count?'

psychological_barriers.trigger: Student who exceeded the 90-day limit describes the DSO 'helping them' and frames the story as resolved
psychological_barriers.underlying_state: Normalization of near-misses — the fact that a consequence was narrowly avoided leads to underweighting the risk for future readers
psychological_barriers.how_it_manifests: Other students read the story and conclude 'DSO will fix it if I mess up' rather than 'this was a serious violation that required intervention'
psychological_barriers.expert_move: Reframe: 'DSO intervention helped in this case, but DSO cannot always fix a violation retroactively — especially if it surfaces during H-1B adjudication. The RFE story in the comments shows what happens when there's no fix available.'

reframing_moves.original_framing: I'm on OPT — can I change jobs?
reframing_moves.reframe: OPT is your work authorization, not your employer's sponsorship. The question isn't whether you can change jobs — you can. The question is how to do it without losing compliance days.
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Eliminates the false premise that OPT is employer-tied (like H-1B), freeing the student to focus on the actual constraint: managing the 90-day unemployment clock
reframing_moves.example_language: OPT是你的工作许可，不是雇主的担保，所以换工作本身是允许的

reframing_moves.original_framing: I only had a 4-month gap — I thought that was okay for OPT
reframing_moves.reframe: The 90-day rule doesn't care what you thought — and it doesn't disappear when OPT ends. It shows up in your H-1B file.
reframing_moves.why_it_works: Shifts the student from present-tense compliance thinking to long-term immigration record thinking; the gap between 'OPT is over' and 'this is permanent' is the hidden trap
reframing_moves.example_language: 后来H-1B RFE被问到这段时间 (the H-1B RFE later asked about this period)
